productIdea: A local-first planning and handoff layer that helps a team take a rough idea and turn it into a build-ready markdown package with gates, scorecards, and step-by-step delivery guidance before implementation starts.
targetAudience: Technical product owners, senior engineers, AI-assisted builders, and delivery teams receiving ambiguous product requests.
problemStatement: Teams often start coding from half-formed ideas, which causes rework, shallow requirements, weak acceptance criteria, and poor handoffs between product, AI coding tools, and engineering teams.
constraints: Keep the MVP local-first, markdown-first, and simple to run with npm install and npm run dev. Avoid auth, payments, and databases unless they become strictly necessary.
desiredOutput: A plan package with a project brief, guided questionnaire, critique, phased gate files, readiness score, step-by-step build guide, and downloadable artifact zip.
mustHaveFeatures: Profile-aware planning flow, guided questionnaire, critique engine, phase plan, gate entry and exit files, readiness scorecard, markdown package preview, zip export, CLI generation from JSON input.
niceToHaveFeatures: Saved drafts, collaboration, template sharing, connector integrations, richer analytics.
dataAndIntegrations: Project brief fields, questionnaire answers, generated markdown artifacts, zip download, local CLI input JSON, future GitHub integration.
risks: Users may still provide shallow answers, overscope v1, skip gate discipline, or misread the score as approval to code blindly.
successMetrics: A user can run the app locally, create a sample project, see the generated phases and gates, download a zip, and run the CLI to produce the same package from input JSON.
nonGoals: No auth, no payments, no production database, no heavy visual polish, no direct prompt authoring.
timeline: Ship a working local MVP first, then validate the workflow before deeper persistence or integrations.
teamContext: One planner fills out the package and then hands it to another AI coding tool or engineering team.

questionnaireAnswers.north-star: The first release must prove that a vague request can become a build-ready package with enough clarity to guide implementation without more back-and-forth.
questionnaireAnswers.primary-workflow: Start a project, choose a profile, provide the raw brief, answer the guided questions, review the critique, inspect phases and gates, then export the package.
questionnaireAnswers.failure-modes: The workflow can fail if the brief is vague, the scope stays too broad, integrations are undefined, or the team skips exit gates.
questionnaireAnswers.scope-cut: Keep the planning workflow, critique, phases, scorecard, export, and CLI. Defer persistence, collaboration, and external connectors.
questionnaireAnswers.acceptance: A skeptical technical reviewer should be able to read the markdown package, understand what to build, and identify the gate conditions and testing expectations.
questionnaireAnswers.operating-risks: Gate skipping, stale markdown artifacts, under-specified integrations, and false confidence from incomplete planning are the key operating risks.
